coredata.dc:title: The aggregation of multiple miR-29a cancer biomarkers induced by graphene quantum dots: Molecular dynamics simulations
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Molecular Graphics and Modelling
coredata.dc:creator: Natmai, Saowalak
Kuntip, Nattapon
Japrung, Deanpen
Pongprayoon, Prapasiri
coredata.dc:description: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small non-coding RNAs that play a role in regulating gene expression. MiRNAs are focused on as potential cancer biomarkers due to their involvement in the cancer development. New effective techniques for extracting miRNA from a biological matrix is important. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used to detect DNA/RNA in many sensor platforms, but the application in miRNA extraction remains limited. To extract miRNAs, the miRNA adsorption and desorption on GQD are the key. Thus, in this work, the adsorption mechanism of excess miRNA on GQD in solution is revealed using Molecular dynamics simulations. The miRNA assemblies on one and two GQDs were studied to explore the possibility of using GQD for miRNA extraction. The folded miR-29a molecule, one of key cancer biomarkers, is used as an miRNA model. Three systems with one (6miR) and two GQDs (with parallel (6miR_2GP) and sandwich (6miR_2GS) organisations) in six-miR-29a solution were set. The data show excess miR-29a can reduce the miR-29a-GQD binding efficiency. The opening of intrabase pairing of GQD-absorbed miR-29a facilitates the interbase coupling resulting in the self-aggregation of miR-29a. The GQD organisation also affects the miR-29a adsorption ability. The additional GQDs result in the tighter miR-29a adsorption which can retard the miR-29a desorption. The proper GQD concentration is thus important to successfully collect all miR-29a and accommodate the easy miR-29a dissociation. Our results can be useful for a design of DNA probe and choosing decent nanosized GRA concentration for experimental setups.
coredata.dcterms:subject: miRNA
miR-29a
Graphene quantum dot
Cancer biomarker
MD simulations

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Molecular Graphics and Modelling

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Cartoon view of miR-29a and its sequence. The hairpin structure is shown in a red rectangle where its tip (U16) is shown in green bead. (B)–(D) show the initial orientations of all systems (6miR, 6miR_2GP, and 6miR_2GS) where the example of whole simulation box is displayed in (B). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Cartoon view of folded miR-29a in a solution. (B)–(D) Final snapshots of each system. The average self-hydrogen bonds are also displayed with standard deviation. Average hydrogen bonds in 6miR, 6miR_2 GS, and 6miR_2GP were calculated from the last 100 ns data, where that of miR-29a in a solution was computed from the last 50 ns.
Number of hydrogen bonds between chains in all systems. The residues involving in the interaction network are shown in (C).
RMSDs of GRA nanosheets in all systems. The orientations of GRA in each system are presented in the insets.
(A)–(E) Frequencies of GRA contacts with each residue in miR-29a. (F) shows the location of each nucleotide.
The average number of water and GRA contacts with standard deviation in all systems. Only contacts within 0.35 nm are counted. The last 100 ns are used for this calculation.

head.ce:title: The aggregation of multiple miR-29a cancer biomarkers induced by graphene quantum dots: Molecular dynamics simulations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok, 10900, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok, 10900, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Nanotechnology Center, National Science and Technology Development Agency, Thailand Science Park, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Science and Technology Development Agency

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Nanotechnology Center, National Science and Technology Development Agency, Thailand Science Park, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Advanced Studied in Nanotechnology for Chemical, Food, and Agricultural Industries, KU Institute for Advanced Studies, Kasetsart University, Bangkok, 10900, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Advanced Studied in Nanotechnology for Chemical

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Advanced Studied in Nanotechnology for Chemical, Food, and Agricultural Industries, KU Institute for Advanced Studies, Kasetsart University, Bangkok, 10900 Thailand

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok, 10900, Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small non-coding RNAs that play a role in regulating gene expression. MiRNAs are focused on as potential cancer biomarkers due to their involvement in the cancer development. New effective techniques for extracting miRNA from a biological matrix is important. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used to detect DNA/RNA in many sensor platforms, but the application in miRNA extraction remains limited. To extract miRNAs, the miRNA adsorption and desorption on GQD are the key. Thus, in this work, the adsorption mechanism of excess miRNA on GQD in solution is revealed using Molecular dynamics simulations. The miRNA assemblies on one and two GQDs were studied to explore the possibility of using GQD for miRNA extraction. The folded miR-29a molecule, one of key cancer biomarkers, is used as an miRNA model. Three systems with one (6miR) and two GQDs (with parallel (6miR_2GP) and sandwich (6miR_2GS) organisations) in six-miR-29a solution were set. The data show excess miR-29a can reduce the miR-29a-GQD binding efficiency. The opening of intrabase pairing of GQD-absorbed miR-29a facilitates the interbase coupling resulting in the self-aggregation of miR-29a. The GQD organisation also affects the miR-29a adsorption ability. The additional GQDs result in the tighter miR-29a adsorption which can retard the miR-29a desorption. The proper GQD concentration is thus important to successfully collect all miR-29a and accommodate the easy miR-29a dissociation. Our results can be useful for a design of DNA probe and choosing decent nanosized GRA concentration for experimental setups.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small endogenous non-coding RNA (approximately 22 nucleotides) found in eukaryotes. They are known as powerful regulators of gene expression. They regulate a wide range of biological processes including carcinogenesis [1,2]. In the circulation, miRNAs exist in very stable forms. The quality and quantity changes of such circulating miRNAs are directly related to the tumor progression and can be detected by molecular techniques [3,4]. Furthermore, the pathology specificity of circulating miRNAs [5,6] makes them become an excellent candidate of emerging cancer biomarker. Thus, many attempts have been made to design and develop novel techniques to extract and detect miRNAs from a biological sample [7]. The efficient miRNA extraction and purification from a biological source are keys for sensitive and effective detection of miRNAs. Many inaccuracies during the miRNA detection can be occurred due to poorly controlled sample preparation and isolation. Traditionally, organic solvents and columns packed with solid sorbents are commonly used for miRNA extraction, however they give a low recovery for miRNAs and do not meet current demands [6,8,9]. Various nanomaterials such as graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, and metal nanoparticles are exploited for DNA and RNA detection [6,8,10–18]. They are used for segregating nucleic acids from interfering molecules in a biological matrix and some are directly complied with detection assays [19,20]. One of key steps for effective DNA and RNA isolation is the adsorption/desorption event of nucleic acid on nanomaterials. Nonetheless, such nanomaterial-based techniques are mostly applied to long single-stranded DNA and RNA [21–23]. The applications of nanomaterials in the short miRNA extraction remain limited. The miRNA recovery is dependent on the efficiency of the extraction method [24,25], therefore the novel isolation methods that provide high quality and quantity of miRNAs recovered are needed.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional nanosized graphene with less than 10 nm in size [11,26]. They have inherent abilities to catalyse redox species, enable charge transfer, and interact with analytes through π–π interactions [27]. GQDs capture an attention in biomedical field due to their tunable photoluminescence properties, high photostability, low cytotoxicity, and high biocompatibility [28–31]. GQDs have been recently employed in luminescence sensors, electrochemical biosensors, optical sensors, and photo-electrochemical biosensors [26]. Comparing to other nanomaterials such as graphene oxide (GO), metal quantum dots, and colloidal semiconductors, GQDs show more biocompatibility and non-toxicity [31–34]. The ability to be a good substrate for biomolecules and quenching agents [35–37] allows the involvement of GQDs in many disease biosensor platforms, including miRNA detection [32,38,39]. Nonetheless, there are insufficient data on exploiting GQDs to extract miRNAs. It is interesting to explore the possibility of using GQDs in miRNA isolation in order to pave a way for new isolation techniques. Even though GO has shown a good performance on the extraction of short RNAs from biological sources [16,40,41], the lower cytotoxicity and more bio-inert properties of GQDs are attractive enough to probe their performances for miRNA isolation. The understanding of how miRNAs adsorb and desorb on GQD in microscopic level is crucial to primarily determine the miRNA-extracting capacity of GQDs. Recently, the adsorption mechanisms of multiple miR-29a cancer biomarker in a presence and absence of its complementary DNAs on a single GQD have been investigated [42,43]. It was found that free area of GQDs can interfere the hybridization between nucleobases which can lead to a false positive signal. Nonetheless, the effect of multiple miRNAs and GQDs on miRNA adsorption was not determined. This insight is useful for experimentalists to prepare sample and GQD substrate for miRNA extraction.

Thus, in this work, Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations were employed to study how multiple miRNAs react with one and two GQDs. A nanosized graphene (GRA) sheet with a dimension of 4.2 nm × 4.9 nm is used as a GQD model. MiR-29a, which is one of potent biomarkers for various cancer types [1,44–46], is used as an miRNA model (Fig. 1 A). Three miR-29a-GRA systems were set and categorised into two groups: (i) single GRA with six miR-29a molecules (6miR) (Fig. 1B) and (ii) two GRAs aligned in parallel (6miR_2GP) and sandwich (6miR_2GS) orientations (Fig. 1C and D). It is known that the position of aqueous GRAs is random. The two GRA poses used here act as a toy model to explore the effect of GRA alignment on miR-29a adhesion. The folded miR-29a was used in this work to demonstrate the effect of its secondary structure on GQD binding. Many early computational studies on the binding of single-stranded DNA/RNA to graphene and its derivatives have been investigated [47–51], but all exploited the non-native single-stranded DNA/RNA structures which cannot reflect their behaviour in a native condition. Only the miR-29a adsorption mechanism is revealed here because the spontaneous desorption process requires a long timescale which is not practical for conventional MD simulations. However, the understanding of the adsorption mechanism here can be useful for future design and development of miRNA extraction approaches.

The starting 3D structures of miR-29a and GRA were obtained from a previous study [52]. MiR-29a forms a hairpin loop at the 3′- end and lets the 5′-end free to move (Fig. 1A). U16 is the tip of hairpin structure. GRA with a dimension of 4.2 × 4.9 n
m 2
were placed at the center of simulation box. Three systems were set: (1) Six miR-29a chains with a single GRA sheet (6miR), (2) Six miR-29a chains with two GRAs aligned in parallel (6miR_2GP), and (3) Six miR-29a with two GRAs aligned in a sandwich orientation (6miR_2GS). The locations of miR-29a chains in all systems are shown in Fig. 1B–D. 6miR, 6miR_2GP, and 6miR_2GS were placed in a simulation box with sizes of 100 x 140 x 120 n
m 3
, 90 x160 x 90 n
m 3
, and 90 x 100 x 150 n
m 3
, respectively. Each miR-29a was placed at least 0.1 nm away from a GRA surface. Each system was neutralized by counter ions and soaked in 1 M NaCl electrolyte solution with TIP3P water molecules. The 10,000 steps of energy minimization were performed with steepest descent algorithm and followed by 10-ns equilibration run. The MD production runs were conducted for 500 ns for 6miR and 1000 ns for 6miR_2GP and 6miR-2GS. Both GRA and miR-29a were set to freely move in order to mimic extraction environment in a sample solution.

GROMACS 2020.1 (www.gromacs.org) [53] was carried out with AMBER99SB-ILDN force field [54]. A harmonic potential with a spring constant of 1000 kJ mol-1
nm
− 2
was employed [55]. The particle mesh Ewald (PME) method [56,57] with a short-range cutoff of 1 nm, Fourier spacing of 0.12 nm, and fourth-order spline interpolation were employed for long-range electrostatic interactions [55]. Bond lengths in each system were constrained by the LINCS method [58]. Periodic boundary conditions were applied in xyz directions. All simulations were conducted with the constant number of particles, pressure and temperature (NPT). MiR-29a, GRA, solvent and ions were coupled individually using the v-scale thermostat [59] at 300 K with a coupling constant τ t = 0.1 ps. The pressure was coupled using Parrinello-Rahman algorithm at 1 bar with a coupling constant τ p = 1 ps. The time step for integration was 2 fs. Coordinates were saved every 2 ps.

GROMACS tools and locally written code were used for data analysis. Graphical figures were generated by VMD [60]. RMSD and RMSF calculations were computed using an initial structure from each production as a reference. The hydrogen bonds were computed using “gmx hbond” with default parameters (The hydrogen-donor-acceptor cutoff angle is 30° and the cutoff radius (X-acceptor) as 0.35 nm). The percentages of contacts are computed using the cutoff of 0.35 nm.

To determine how miR-29a chains interact with GRA surface, a number of water and GRA contacts are calculated (Table 1 ). It is shown in Table 1 that miR-29a are highly water-exposed (∼2700–3400 water contacts). In a previous work [52], four miR-29a molecules can be absorbed on a single nanosized GRA and each miR-29a absorbate has no interaction with each other. In contrast, six miR-29a molecules in this work (6miR) seem to cause the saturation of GRA since the self-aggregation is captured. Chain A in 6miR seems to be shielded from GRA by coupling with chains B and F (Table 1, Figs. 2B and S1 in supplementary information). Each miR-29a molecule in a previous work makes ∼200–400 GRA contacts where the tightest GRA binding is due to the clipping conformation of absorbate [52]. Here, no clipping conformation is captured in 6miR. A presence of two more miR-29a molecules in 6miR significantly interferes the miRNA-GRA interaction network. MiR-29a molecules require ∼20 to 158 GRA contacts depending on their GRA-bound conformations (Table 1). Some absorbates can stand on a GRA surface by either two legs (the 5′- and 3′- termini) or the tip of its hairpin structure (U16) (Figs. 2B and S1 in supplementary information). Chains B and C show the least GRA contacts because they stay upright on a GRA surface by their U16. Chain D generates the most GRA contacts because of the laying down of its 5′-terminus (Figs. 2B and S1 in supplementary information). Our results agree with a recent work [52] that the miR-29a-GRA assembly is fast and spontaneous. However, it is curious that why the clamping pose of miR-29a is not found in a higher miR-29a concentration in 6miR. Besides, the miR-29a-GRA contacts are also reduced. Having more electronegative miR-29a molecules in a system may change the electrostatic environment that affects the morphology of bound miR-29a on GRA. Not only the
π
–
π
interactions between GRA and bases, but other factors such as miR-29a concentration may also involve in the conformation of miR-29a absorbate. Comparing with a previous miR-29a-GRA work [52], more miR-29a chains here show less GRA contacts which implies the looser miR-29a binding. This may facilitate the extraction when the complementary probe is added.

In case of 6miR_2GP and 6miR_2GS, two different poses of GRAs were set (parallel and sandwich conformations in Fig. 1C and D). These GRA organisations serve as a toy model to determine how GRA distribution affects the efficiency of collecting miR-29a in a solution. Seemingly, adding more GRA sheets in 6miR_2GP and 6miR_2GS can accommodate the tighter miR-29a adsorption (GRA contacts increase up to ∼300 contacts/chain/2GRAs) (Table 1). The different GRA alignments (parallel and sandwich) promote the diverse miR-29a-GRA assemblies. Although the miR-29a-GRA aggregation is captured in both cases (6miR_2GP and 6miR_2GS), both GRA organisations provide dissimilar abilities to collect miR-29a. The parallel GRA lining (6miR_2GP) seems to show more potential to absorb miR-29a than 6miR_2GS. The compact GRA-miR-29a lump in 6miR_2GP may induce the better nucleation. In 6miR_2GP, chains D and E are found to form contacts with both GRAs due to their intercalation between GRA1 and GRA2, whereas the rest mostly sit on either GRA1 or GRA2 (Table 1 and Fig. 2 ). Similarly, chains D and E in 6miR_2GS also serve as a linker between GRA1 and GRA2 (Table 1 and Fig. 2). In addition, a smaller number of GRA contacts with chains B (∼47 contacts) and C (∼89 contacts) in 6miR_2GS are because of the standing conformation of these chains (Fig. 2).

In Fig. 2, the self-hydrogen bonds of miR-29a molecules in each system are computed to determine the stability of secondary structure upon the GRA binding. MiR-29a in an aqueous solution requires ∼22.97 self-hydrogen bonds to maintain its secondary structure (Fig. 2). When miR-29a molecules bind to GRA, they lose their intra-hydrogen bonds causing the denaturation of their secondary structure. This nucleotide damage by GQDs has also been reported in a previous work [34]. The self-hydrogen bonds are decreased to ∼7–13 hydrogen bonds depending on the GRA-binding poses. Among all, six GRA-bound miR-29a orientations are identified where: (1) the whole miR-29a lays down on a GRA sheet; (2) the hairpin region aligns on a GRA surface and let the 5′-terminal to the bulk; (3) the tip of hairpin loop (U16) and the 5′-tail adhere on GRA; (4) the whole miR-29a chain clips on GRA; (5) miR-29a stands on GRA by the tip of hairpin (U16); (6) both 3′- and 5′- tails stick on GRA and let the rest expose to the bulk (Fig. S2 in supplementary information). The latter two poses are found to be crucial for linking two GRAs in 6miR_2GP and 6miR_2GS. The free terminus or U16 can further form the interactions with the adjacent GRA resulting in the miRNA-GRA clustering (Fig. 2). Not only interacting with GRA, miR-29a chains can also interact with other chains. In 6miR, chain A is found to aggregate on top of chains B and F by base-backbone and backbone-backbone hydrogen bonds (Fig. 3 A). This also reflects the saturation of GRA surface binding sites. Furthermore, the interbase stacking conformation in Fig. 3A also suggests the contribution of interbase coupling in this interaction network. Both
π
–
π
stacking and hydrogen bonds also serve as keys for chain-chain interactions in 6miR_2GP and 6miR_2GS (Fig. 3B and C). When adhering on GRA, miR-29a loses some secondary structure which causes the base-pair opening. This scenario allows free nucleobases to interact with nearby sugar-phosphate backbone and bases via hydrogen bonds and interbase coupling (Fig. 3). MiR-29a can bind the adjacent unit by the GRA-unbound regions which are the top area of hairpin structure and both tails (Fig. 3). These unbound parts can also serve as a probe recognition site.

Apart from the unwinding of miR-29a secondary structures, the binding of miR-29a also induces the GRA conformational change. The Root Mean Square Deviations (RMSDs) are computed to determine the GRA flexibilities in each system (Fig. 4 ). In Fig. 4, a sudden shift of RMSD in 6miR is found due to the curvy GRA where all miR-29a molecules are clustered on one face of GRA (Figs. 2B and 4A). The effects of bound miR-29a on the GRA bending are weakened in di-GRA systems (6miR_2GP and 6miR_2GS). No U-shaped GRA structure is captured in 6miR_2GP and 6miR_2GS (Fig. 2D and E). Only wrinkle GRA conformations are found (Fig. 4). This is due to most of miR-29a chains are aligned on both GRA faces which enhances the GRA rigidity. Both curvy and wrinkle poses of mobilised GRA were also observed in previous studies [37,61–63]. However, the miRNA assembly on mobilised GRA in an aqueous solution has high degrees of freedom. This work thus displays some of possible complex conformations that can be formed. In addition, the miR-29a-GRA cluster in 6miR_2GP seems to pack more tightly than that of 6miR_2GS resulting in more consistent RMSDs of GRAs (Fig. 2C, D, 4B, and 4C). The quick GRA1-GRA2 intercalation is formed before they separate in 6miR_2GS (Fig. 4C). Seemingly, the arrangement of GRAs appears to promote different miR-29a aggregation efficiency. It is known that the GRA position in a solution is unpredictable. The parallel and sandwich alignments of GRAs in this work are examples of possible GRA poses that may be occurred in a solution. It is unexpected that different GRA arrangements can vary the miR-29a adsorption conformation. However, this finding can be one of possible GRA-miRNA binding poses in a solution. The effects of other factors such as GRA shape, dimension, and concentration on GRA-miRNA clustering need to be studied further. This is a pioneer work on the influence of multiple GRAs on a miRNA adhesion. This molecular finding can be useful for future design of novel miRNA extraction strategies. Furthermore, a small nucleotide chain like miR-29a seems to act as a linker to persuade the nanosized GRA aggregation. A presence of miR-29a can block the GRA-GRA stacking.

Considering the contact position of miR-29a on GRA, as reported earlier in the text, miR-29a molecules in 6miR employ their both tails (Residues 1–8 and 22) and the top moiety of hairpin loop (residues 15–17) to adhere on GRA (Fig. 5 A and F). These GRA-absorbed regions are in a good agreement with a previous work [52]. For 6miR_2GS and 6miR_2GP, the parallel organisation of GRA seems to induce better miR-29a-GRA clustering. In 6miR_2GP, various parts of miR-29a molecules can attach on GRA surface (Fig. 5). GRA1 and GRA2 are linked by chains D and E (Fig. 2). These chains employ both termini and hairpin loop to bridge two GRAs (Figs. 2 and S1 in supplementary information). This linkage is also found in 6miR_2GS. GRAs in 6miR_2GP seem to pile up, whereas those in 6miR_2GS are likely to line up (Fig. 2). The GRA1-GRA2 orientation in 6miR_2GS generates more free GRA surface binding sites resulting in the spreading of miR-29a over GRA faces (Fig. 2). Moreover, the 5′-tail acts as the main contributor for anchoring miR-29a on GRA (Fig. 5D and F). Seemingly, more free GRA surface binding sites in 6miR_2GS generate similar miR-29a binding mechanism to 6miR. Except chain A where all residues at the 5′-tail are absorbed on GRA (Figs. 2 and 5), miR-29a chains in 6miR and 6miR_2GS seem to share similar GRA-binding sites which are the beginning part of 5′-tail and the hairpin head. Similar to previous DNA/RNA-graphene studies [12,43,47,51,52,64], the
π − π
bonding between GRA and nucleobase is the main contributor for miRNA adsorption here (Table S1 in supplementary information). The backbone-GRA interactions are rare here. The extra GRAs fail to induce more GRA-miRNA contacts. In contrast, when binding to GRA, the opening up of base-base pairing allows the inter-chain interactions. This scenario was not seen in a previous miRNA-GRA system [52]. It seems that the ratio of miR-29a to GRA influences the binding conformations and consequently the miR-29a assembly. A high concentration of miR-29a can reduce the adsorption on GRA.

Combining with a previous work [52], it can be suggested that the adsorption efficiency of each miRNA is unique depending on its secondary structures. This difference can benefit the release of GRA-bound miRNAs. In experiment, the high concentration of urea is used to elute all GRA-bound miRNAs to a medium [16]. This method causes the complete desorption of all nucleotides. On the contrary, it is interesting to use low urea concentration to weaken the GRA-miRNA interactions of loosely bound miRNA in order for an easy access of complementary DNA probes.

Herein, the morphological organisation of miR-29a on graphene (GRA) quantum dot in aqueous solution, using the folded miR-29a model, are examined. The aim is to examine the possibility of using graphene quantum dot to extract miR-29a molecules from a biological matrix. The effects of multiple miR-29a and GRAs are determined here. It is found that the aggregation of miR-29a and nanosized GRA is fast and spontaneous which agrees well with previous studies [12,22,43,65]. Comparing with a previous miR-29a-GRA work [52], upon a miR-29a adhesion, the additional miR-29a molecules (6miR) do not only reduce the adsorption efficiency of all miR-29a on GRA, but also induce the self-aggregation due to the opening of base-base pairing. This event also reflects the saturated GRA surface. Such self-assembly can accommodate the easy miR-29a desorption. The alignment of GRA nanosheets in a solution also affects the miR-29a adsorption. The additional free GRA surfaces promote the stronger miR-29a adhesion which may cause the difficulty for miR-29 desorption. The use of proper GRA concentration is important. Too high GRA concentration can lower down the desorption efficiency. Our results can be useful for a design of DNA probe and choosing decent nanosized GRA concentration for experimental setups.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript Funding Sources.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Kasetsart University Research and Development Institute (Grant no. FF(KU)24.65) and National Nanotechnology Center (NANOTEC, Grant no. P1861652 and P2150900) for financial supports. This work was also partially supported by the scholarship of the National Science and Technology Development Agency (NSTDA) in conformance with the Graduate Study Strategy in Technology and Science (No. SCA-CO-2562-9727-TH) and Kasetsart University Integrated Chemistry (KUIC) program.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmgm.2022.108267.
